sources.short: Connecticut Society of Colonial Wars Pedigree

narrative_blocks.header: Came with Rev. Peter Prudden's company from Hertfordshire
narrative_blocks.body: Came with Rev. Peter Prudden's company from Hertfordshire, England, arriving Boston July 26, 1637
Among original settlers of Wepawaug (Milford); removed to New Haven ca.1641

narrative_blocks.header: Freeman 1639; judge in New Milford 1640; Oathe
narrative_blocks.body: Freeman 1639; judge in New Milford 1640; Oathe of Fidelity administered by Gov. Theophilus Eaton July 1, 1644

narrative_blocks.header: Appointed clerk to New Haven Artillery Company 1648
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed clerk to New Haven Artillery Company 1648

narrative_blocks.header: Deacon of First Church New Haven 1656–1667
narrative_blocks.body: Deacon of First Church New Haven 1656–1667

Helped build the Phantom Ship — the New Haven vessel whose loss in 1647 took Thomas Gregson and other leading citizens. His name is memorialized on Founder's Bridge over the Wepawaug River in Milford, CT.

narrative_blocks.header: He refused the King’s ship money
narrative_blocks.body: The 1636 Hertfordshire arrears roll lists Richard Miles of Wormley among those who would not pay Charles I’s ship-money tax — with the notation “gone into New England.” He landed at Boston the next summer with Rev. Peter Prudden’s Hertfordshire company.

narrative_blocks.header: He helped build the phantom ship
narrative_blocks.body: Governor Eaton entrusted Miles and others with constructing New Haven’s great ship of 1646 — the ill-built vessel that sailed into the Atlantic and was never seen again. Among the lost was Thomas Gregson, whose daughter later married into Hartford’s Whiting family.

narrative_blocks.header: One of Milford’s forty-four free planters
narrative_blocks.body: At Milford’s first General Court in November 1639, forty-four church members were granted the franchise as “free planters,” Miles among them. A 1641 New Haven schedule shows him heading a household of seven on four hundred acres.

bio.notes: Also spelled "Myles" in early records. Parents: Rychard and Alice Cherrye Myles.

sources.short: Connecticut Society of Colonial Wars Pedigree

parents.father_research_notes: Richard Miles — New Haven founder, came with Rev. Peter Prudden 1637

military_service.notes: Ordered Sept 9, 1675 to stay at Westfield with 26 men in his charge. 7th in command of Connecticut Forces Nov 1675.
Appointed Captain of New Haven County Dragoons Apr 1690 for service against French and Indians. Captain of New Haven Trainband May 1690. Captain of 150 men raised for Relief of Albany Feb 21, 1693.

narrative_blocks.body: In September 1675, as King Philip’s War burned through the valley towns, the Council ordered Miles to stay at Westfield with twenty-six men in his charge. By November he was a lieutenant and seventh in command of all Connecticut forces.

narrative_blocks.header: One hundred fifty men for Albany
narrative_blocks.body: Fifteen years later he was still serving — captain of the New Haven County Dragoons against the French and Indians in 1690, and in February 1693 captain of the hundred and fifty men raised for the Relief of Albany.

bio.bio_blurb: Milford man who died on Christmas Day, 1711

narrative_blocks.header: He died on Christmas Day at twenty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel Miles, son of Captain John Miles of New Haven, married Sarah Newton around 1707 and fathered two sons before his death on Christmas Day, 1711, at just twenty-eight. He was buried in New Haven’s Grove Street ground.

narrative_blocks.header: His widow remarried into the Ingersolls
narrative_blocks.body: After Daniel’s early death, Sarah Newton married Jonathan Ingersoll of Milford and had five more children, knitting the Miles and Ingersoll families into the same Hooker-descended line.
